Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


000
001
      X T RANSPLANT : A P ROBE INTO THE U PPER B OUND
002   P ERFORMANCE OF M ULTILINGUAL C APABILITY IN
003
004   LLM S VIA C ROSS - LINGUAL T RANSPLANTATION
005
006
       Anonymous authors
007
       Paper under double-blind review
008
009
010
011                                                A BSTRACT
012
013            Current large language models (LLMs) often display significant imbalances in
014            their multilingual capabilities and cultural adaptability, primarily due to their un-
015            balanced and English-centric pretraining data. For these English-centric LLMs,
016            the disparities between English and non-English languages hinder their ability to
017            utilize their robust English-based capabilities within non-English contexts, while
018
               also limiting access to valuable multilingual knowledge derived from non-English
               “language-specific neurons” within English contexts. Motivated by this, our work
019
               explores the possibility for LLMs to leverage the strengths of both English and
020
               non-English languages, aiming to further unlock their multilingual potential. To
021            this end, we propose a probing method named X Transplant, which directly trans-
022            plants feed-forward activations from English input to non-English (or from non-
023            English to English) during inference stage, allowing the model to benefit from
024            both English and additional multilingual knowledge. Through extensive exper-
025            iments on our pilotsets and representative LLMs across different tasks and lan-
026            guages, we empirically prove that both the multilingual capabilities and cul-
027            tural adaptability of LLMs hold the potential to be significantly improved by the
028            cross-lingual feed forward transplantation, respectively from En → non-En and
029
               non-En → En. Additionally, we also establish the upper bound performance
               of LLMs obtained through X Transplant (relative growth of +80% in multilingual
030
               capabilities, +39% in cultural adaptability), highlighting the underutilization of
031
               current LLMs’ multilingual potential. We do hope our further analysis and dis-
032            cussion could suggest promising directions for deeply unlocking the multilingual
033            potential of current English-centric LLMs.
034
035
036
      1    I NTRODUCTION
037
038
039
      In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have showcased their remarkable versatility across
      a wide range of downstream tasks (Zhao et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Dong et al., 2023; Wei et al.,
040
      2022a;b; Shanahan, 2022), as well as their evident generalizability and adaptability in multilin-
041
      gual scenarios. However, the significant imbalances in their multilingual capabilities and cultural
042   adaptability still remain challenges that researchers are striving to resolve (Ye et al., 2023; Li et al.,
043   2024a; Shi et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024). These issues primarily stem from their unbalanced training
044   corpora, which is predominantly in English, leading to these models being termed English-centric
045   LLMs (Brown et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023; Biderman et al., 2023).
046
      Existing methods for these challenges primarily focus on Multilingual Pretraining and Cross-lingual
047
      Transfer. Multilingual Pretraining involves initially or continuously training models on diverse mul-
048   tilingual datasets to develop an overall improvement of their multilingual capabilities (Lin et al.,
049   2021; Scao et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024b). While Cross-lingual Transfer leverages
050   knowledge from high-resource languages to enhance the performance of low-resource languages
051   through fine-tuning techniques (Reid & Artetxe, 2022; Cahyawijaya et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2023;
052   Khurana et al., 2024). However, these training-based methods have shown potential limitations.
053   Conneau et al. (2020) identified the “curse of multilinguality”, a form of negative interference (Wang
      et al., 2020), where expanding too much languages during pretraining eventually leads to a decline.

089    English to benefit from non-English knowledge). By leveraging this probe, our study delves into two                                                                          5
090    distinct avenues: the impact of En → non-En transplantation on LLMs’ multilingual5 capabilities,                                                                                   Figure 2: Sa
                                                                                                                             Figure 2: Sample figure caption.
091    and how non-En → En transplantation affects LLMs’ non-English cultural adaptability.
092    We conduct extensive experiments and analysis on four representive LLMs across the pilotsets of
093    three multilingual datasets and one culture-aware dataset. By assessing the upper bound perfor-
094    mance of LLMs obtained through exhaustively evaluating all possible settings of X Transplant, we
095    empirically demonstrate that X Transplant hold the potential to push the boundaries of what LLMs
096    can typically achieve in multilingual and culture-aware tasks, with an average upper bound 30%
097    higher than the original, which highlights the underutilization of current LLMs’ multilingual poten-
098    tial. We also undertake some analysis and discussion concerning further unlocking LLMs’ multilin-
099    gual potential, with the hope of providing insights for future research.
100
101     2      BACKGROUND AND H YPOTHESIS
102
103    In this section, we provide a detailed overview of the background that motivates our research and
104    clearly articulate the central hypothesis underpinning our proposed probing approach.
105
106    Decoder-only Large Language Models The transformer-based GPT series of models have shown
107    remarkable effectiveness in natural language generation (Radford et al., 2018; Brown et al., 2020),
       triggering a boom around LLMs. Within Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), the feed-forward


                                                                                                        2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      layers and self-attention module constitute the main body of a decoder block for current LLMs. Let
109   x denote the input matrix, a decoder-only layer can be mainly formulated as follows:
110
                  AttRes(x) = Concat(Self-Att-head1 (x), . . . , Self-Att-headh (x))WO + x             (1)
111
112                           DecoderBlock(x) = FFN(AttRes(x)) + AttRes(x)                             (2)
113   where Self-Att-headh (x) represents a single attention head, Concat concatenates all heads followed
114
      by a projection with WO , and AttRes(x) is the intermediate state obtained by adding a residual
      connection to the projected heads. The feed-forward module is denoted by FFN. The whole decoder
115
      output, is obtained by applying a residual connection between AttRes(x) and the output of FFN.
116
117   The background, that the feed-forward layers have been shown in many studies to play a crucial role
118   in storing factual knowledge (Geva et al., 2021; Dai et al., 2022; Meng et al., 2022), provides the
119   reason for why we explore cross-lingual transplantation on feed-forward layers, which aligns with
120
      our goal of enabling LLMs to fully leverage both English and non-English multilingual knowledge.
121
      Language-specific Neurons The intriguing capability of LLMs to understand and generate text
122
      in various languages is attributed to a subset of neurons within their architecture that exhibit high
123
      activity levels when processing specific languages. Termed as “language-specific neurons”, these
124   components have been identified as pivotal to the multilingual competencies of LLMs (Tang et al.,
125   2024; Kojima et al., 2024). Furthermore, the proportion of these neurons is notably small rela-
126   tive to the entire neural network, yet their targeted activation or deactivation significantly impacts
127   the model’s ability to understand and generate language-specific content (Zhao et al., 2024). This
128   finding has profound implications for enhancing LLMs’ multilingual capabilities.
129
      Building on the theoretical foundations regarding feed-forward layers and language-specific neu-
130   rons, we boldly hypothesize that sharing and transferring feed-forward activations between English
131   and non-English languages may allow the model to leverage the strengths of both language groups.
132   This capacity to integrate advantages from diverse linguistic backgrounds serves as the foundation
133   of our probing method—X Transplant.
134
135
      3     X T RANSPLANT : C ROSS - LINGUAL T RANSPLANTATION
136
137
      In this section, we will present the formulation of X Transplant, elaborate on its implementation
138   details, and delineate several relevant concepts.
139
140   3.1    M ETHODOLOGY
141
142   For a model M with N decoder layers, given an original input xs in source language S, the xs
143   undergoes a forward propagation through all decoder layers to predict the next token. Let the output
144   activations of these N decoders be denoted as Os = {oks }N  k=1 , where each os is obtained by combin-
                                                                                    k

145   ing the feed-forward activations fs and self-attention activations as through a residual connection
                                         k                                  k

146   (as shown in Equation 2). Similarly, for another version of xs in target language T , denoted as xt ,
147   we also have Ot = {okt }Nk=1 with corresponding {ft }k=1 and {at }k=1 . Without any modifications,
                                                            k N           k N

148
      they would predict the first new token ŷs and ŷt with the unembed matrix Wunembed as follows:
149                   ŷs = softmax(Wunembed · oN                          N    N
                                                s ) = softmax(Wunembed · (as + fs ))                       (3)
150                   ŷt = softmax(Wunembed · oN                          N    N
                                                t ) = softmax(Wunembed · (at + ft ))                       (4)
151   Our approach, X Transplant, refines the process by transplanting the feed-forward activations from
152
      the ith decoder layer with input xs to the j th decoder layer with input xt . Formally, ftj is replaced
153   with fsi and the forward propagation of prompting xt then continues with this modification. As a
154   result of this operation, the original {okt }N                                                        j
                                                   k=j will be altered into {õt }k=j due to the update in ft ,
                                                                               k N
155                                           (modified)
      leading to new prediction outcomes ŷt             as follows:
156
157                                    ŷt(modified) = softmax(Wunembed · õN
                                                                            t )                           (5)
158   Notably, X Transplant currently considers only the substitution of feed-forward activations from a
159   single layer, meaning that the aforementioned ith layer and j th layer both refer to a certain, single
160   decoder layer. Besides, X Transplant performs the transplantation only during the forward propaga-
161   tion for predicting the first new token; all subsequent tokens are generated iteratively after the first
      one, without any additional transplantation operations.


                                                         3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
      3.2   M UTUAL T RANSPLANTATION
163
164   Section 3.1 details how X Transplant facilitates the transfer of feed forward activations from lan-
165   guage S to language T . But X Transplant actually supports transplantation in two directions. When
166   prompting in non-English, the feed-forward activations from English can be leveraged to help the
167   process of non-English prompting. Similarly, under the English prompting conditions, the feed-
168   forward activations from non-English languages can be leveraged to help. Specifically, our experi-
169   ments explore the dual attempt of X Transplant: En → non-En and non-En → En.
170
171   3.3   I NSTANCE - WARE U PPER B OUND
172
      For a model M with N decoder layers, both the source layer and target layer selections in
173
      X Transplant offer N possible choices, resulting in N 2 potential transplantation combinations. For
174   a dataset D of a certain size, we conducted X Transplant for each sample across all N 2 possibilities,
175   selecting the optimal solution for each instance. The model’s optimal performance on this dataset,
176   derived from this process, is referred to as the instance-aware upper bound.
177
      We denote MSi →Tj (x) as the output of model M towards question x after applying X Transplant
178
179
      from ith layer of language S to the j th layer of language T . Let ytrue represents the gold answer
      of question x and I(·) is a indicator function that equals 1 if the condition is true, 0 otherwise. The
180
      upper bound performance can be formulated as follows:
181                                                 X
182                  UpperBoundS→T (M, D) =                 max       I(MSi →Tj (x) = ytrue )              (6)
                                                             i,j∈{1,...,N }
183                                                    x∈D

184   Although enumerating all N 2 possibilities is inherently time-consuming, our goal is to benchmark
185   the upper bound of the model’s capabilities achievable through X Transplant, and demonstrate the
186   underlying mechanisms by enumerating all these results.
187
188   4     E XPERIMENTS
189
190   4.1   E XPERIMENTAL S ETUP
191
192   Models. We selected 4 typical LLMs for our experiments and analysis. (1) LLaMA-2-7B-Chat,
193   (2) Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, (3) Qwen2-7B-Instruct, the three representative English-centric mod-
194
      els are employed in our main experiments. (4) Chinese-Alpaca-2-7B, the alpaca-2 model further
      pretrained incrementally on large-scale Chinese data, are used for subsequent further analysis.
195
196
      Datasets. We mainly conduct experiments on 4 benchmarks, which can be categorized into:
197
198   • Multilingual Capability: (1) XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), a natural language inference corpus
199     covering 15 languages, (2) XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), a question answering dataset cover-
200     ing 12 languages, and (3) XCOPA (Ponti et al., 2020), a causal commonsense reasoning dataset
201     covering 11 languages. These datasets consist of linguistically parallel questions designed to
202     assess the model’s ability across languages. For questions in non-English languages, we apply
203     En → non-En X Transplant to harness feed-forward activations from English.
204   • Cultural Adaptability: GlobalOpinionQA contains questions and answers from cross-national
205
        surveys designed to capture diverse opinions on global issues across different countries, all in
        English. This dataset aims to evaluate the model’s cultural adaptability within an English context.
206
        For these questions in English, we apply non-En → En X Transplant, hoping the model to
207
        leverage feed-forward activations from non-English languages to better capture cultural nuances.
208
209   Notably, due to the extensive scale of our experiments1 , we did not use the full size of the datasets
210   mentioned. For each dataset, we used random.seed(666) to randomly sample 50 instances in
211   each language involved, creating our testbed. These smaller yet linguistically balanced datasets are
212   referred to as pilotsets. Detailed information of each pilotset can be found in Appendix B.1.
213      1
           To obtain the instance-aware upper bound of X Transplant, we perform inference on all N 2 possible source
214   and target layer selection strategies for each instance in the dataset (for example, in LLaMA-2-7B-Chat with
215   layer numbers N = 32, N 2 = 1024 times inference are conducted for each instance). Our main experiments
      involves 3 LLMs and 4 pilotset datasets, resulting in over 800 hours of computation on 8 * A800-SXM4-80GB.

251
      Evaluations & Hyperparameters. We evaluate all benchmarks using accuracy metric (details in
252
      Appendix B.2). And as mentioned in Section 3.1, X Transplant transplants a single layer only when
253   predicting the first new token. We use greedy decoding with a max of 20 new tokens for each model.
254
255   Comparative Setup. We compare the UpperBound performance achieved by X Transplant with
256   (1) the original performance of LLMs, (2) PIM (Mu et al., 2024), which concatenates prompts in
257   two languages to activate more neurons and enhance multilingual potential, (3) CoT (Wei et al.,
258   2022b) (results are provided in Appendix B.4), which prompts the models with step-by-step reason-
259   ing to unlock its potential, and (4) Multilingual SFT, which boosts multilingual capabilities
260   by additional multilingual supervised fine-tuning. The implementation details are in Appendix B.3.
261
262   4.2       M AIN R ESULTS
263
264
      The main results of the multilingual datasets are presented in Table 1 and the results for the cultural
      dataset are illustrated in Figure 2. Notably, while we presented both the baseline results and the
265
      upper bound results of X Transplant in the same table or figure, our goal is not to demonstrate the
266
      superiority of X Transplant. Instead, we aim to use these comparisons to illustrate the extent to
267   which multilingual capabilities can be unlocked through the X Transplant mechanism without
268   modifying LLM itself. The following are our observations:
269
          2
              The explanation of accuracy in English subset of XCOPA for Qwen2-7B-Instruct is in Appendix B.5.


                                                                              5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      (1) Underutilization of current LLMs’ multilingual potential. The results show that the upper-
271   bound performance of X Transplant is mostly much higher than the LLMs’ original performance
272   and even applying PIM method by simply concatenating multilingual prompts can result in over a
273   15% improvement on datasets like XNLI and XCOPA, as observed with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3.
274   These results all clearly indicate that the performance of the three representative English-centric
275   LLMs is not fully realized in multilingual or culture-aware tasks, yet they hold significant potential
276   for breakthroughs with some interventions.
277
278   (2) Surprisingly high upper bound performance achievable through X Transplant mechanism.
279   Through an exhaustive search within the N 2 answer space of X Transplant, we established the upper
280   bound performance of X Transplant using Equation 6, as shown in Table 1 and Figure 2. Compared
281
      to the original performance of involved LLMs, X Transplant exhibits surprisingly high upper bounds
      with a average relative increase of +80% in multilingual tasks and +39% in culture-aware task,
282
      which indicates that the LLMs’ multilingual capability and cultural adaptability hold the potential
283
      to be significantly enhanced with the intervention of feed-forward activations from other languages.
284   And the comparison with Multilingual SFT shows that the cross-lingual latent representation
285   interaction enabled by X Transplant not only offers substantial benefits but also has a strong chance
286   of surpassing the improvements achieved through additional supervised fine-tuning, demonstrating
287   an innovative and highly promising direction for extending the boundaries of LLM performance.
288
289   (3) Improvements under English2English setting. In Table 1, we observe that X Transplant also
290   yields performance gain under the English2English setting, which seems inconsistent with the idea
291   that the benefits of X Transplant stem from cross-lingual interactions. However, this result is logical.
292   In this setting, X Transplant simplifies to replacing the feed-forward activations between different de-
293   coder layers within the same input. Since different decoder layers of LLMs capture distinct features
294   of the input and activate different neurons (i.e., knowledge), the          transplanting
                                                                              Under  review as a conferenceoperation
                                                                                                              paper at ICLRbetween
                                                                                                                              2025


295
      these layers can strengthen feature propagation and encourage feature reuse, leading to perfor-
      mance improvements. This phenomenon is analogous to theUnder     dense connections in DenseNet (Huang
296                                                                   216 review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
      et al., 2017), which has been shown to enhance feature flow217and overall
                                                                              by the modelperformance.

297                                                                                                                                  218         answers from each dataset while reducing the inference time cost.

                     tilingualfrom    English       can this

                               alignment   and preserves              Figure
                                                               alignment during3:pretraining.
                                                                                    The upper   Gaobound       results
                                                                                                      et al. (2024)        of English- and
                                                                                                                     explored
362   As shown in Figure 3, we find that for both Chinese-centric LLM achieved by X Transplant
363                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     5
      English- and Chinese-centric LLMs, the feed- from   5   English (UpperBoundEn2lang ) and Chi-
364   forward activations from either English or Chi- nese (UpperBoundZh2lang ). The horizontal line
365   nese results in upper bound result that far ex- represents the model’s original performance.
366   ceeds the LLMs’ original performance. This
367   outcome aligns with our expectations for X Transplant, as it leverages the knowledge activated in
368   one language to assist another, without being confined to English as the sole source language.
369
370   Native language preference in Native-centric LLMs. Figure 3 further reveals that, for LLaMA-
371   2-7B-Chat, the English-centric LLM, activations from English result in a higher upper bound in
372   X Transplant than those from Chinese (En: 74.8%, Zh: 72.9% in average). Meanwhile, in Chinese-
373   Alpaca-2-7B, the Chinese-centric LLM, activations from Chinese can offer greater improvements
374   (En: 63.7%, Zh: 66.3% in average). This indicates a preference in native-centric models, where
375
      feed-forward activations from the model’s native language tend to yield more substantial gains,
      likely because the model’s internal knowledge is more closely aligned with its native language.

396
                           either the source or target layer is fixed. The median in the legend represents the model’s original
397                        performance; thus, red indicates better performance, while blue indicates worse performance.
398
399
                            5.3                     A NALYSIS OF L AYER -S PECIFIC E FFECTIVENESS IN S OURCE AND TARGET S ELECTION
400
401                        In X Transplant, the selection of source and target layers for the transplantation operation is un-
402                        doubtedly a critical issue. The upper bound results in our main experiments were obtained by
403                        exploring all possible combinations of N 2 source and target layer. However, for practical appli-
404                        cation of X Transplant, an instance-level dynamic strategy for selecting source and target layers is
405                        essential. Therefore, we also analyze the impact of different source and target layer selections on
406                        X Transplant. Part of the results are illustrated in Figure 4, while additional results across more mod-
407
                           els and datasets can be found in the Appendix C.2. The layer-specific upper bounds, where either
                           the source or target layer is fixed, are also presented in the figures.
408
409
                           Limited improvement with fixed layer selections: The necessity of an instance-aware strategy.
410
                           As shown in Figure 4, while some minor improvements can be observed under certain settings,
411
                           fixed strategies for selecting source and target layers generally do not yield satisfactory results. This
412                        underscores the necessity for an instance-aware strategy, where appropriate source and target layers
413                        are selected for each instance, to approach or even achieve the overall upper bound performance.
414
415                        Activations from the last layer provide the greatest benefit, and applying X Transplant ear-
416                        lier in the target language yields higher upper bound. From the perspective of source layer
417                        selection, the results in Figure 4 demonstrate that, though selecting the last layer as the source for
418                        X Transplant often results in lower accuracy compared to the model’s original performance, it leads
419                        to the highest upper bound. This suggests that the last layer likely contains significant multilingual
420                        knowledge beneficial to X Transplant, but on an instance-level, the effectiveness of these activations
421
                           largely depends on the selection of the target layer. From the perspective of target layer selection,
                           we found that applying X Transplant earlier in the inference stage of the target language results in
422
                           a higher upper bound performance. This might be because it allows sufficient forward propagation
423
                           space for the model to integrate knowledge from other languages, rather than having knowledge
424                        from other languages dominate at the final stages of inference.
425
426                        Narrowing the N 2 search space to N . For practical application of X Transplant on each instance,
427                        selecting the appropriate source layer and target layer from the N 2 choice space seems challenging.
428                        However, the layer-specific results provide two alternative strategies: either fix the source layer as
429                        the last layer and select the target layer from N options, or fix the target layer as the first layer and
430                        choose the source layer from N options. These two approaches significantly reduce the complexity
431                        of source and target layer selection from N 2 to N while still approaching the overall upper bound
                           performance (original: 45.7%, source-last: 66.3%, target-first:68.2%, overall: 76.8% in average).


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
      6        D ISCUSSION : U NPACKING W HAT I S B EHIND X T RANSPLANT
433
434
      6.1            X T RANSPLANT IS A R ELIABLE AND S TABLE ACTIVATION M ODIFICATION M ECHANISM
435
436   From the perspective of language modeling, the essence of “X Transplant affect model’s output”
437   lies in the fact that certain intermediate states within the model’s inference process are altered,
438   which in turn affects the probability distribution of the next token prediction. This mechanism
439   is fundamentally similar to some approaches in fields such as model editing (Yao et al., 2023;
440   Zhang et al., 2024) and controllable text generation (Liang et al., 2024; Konen et al., 2024).
441
442   Generally, directly modifying activations within a                                                                                    XNLI_llama
                                                                                                                                                                                                                     LLaMA-2-7B-Chat

443   model’s inference process is a delicate operation that, if                                                                                                                                                     Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3

                                                                                                                                                                                                                     Qwen2-7B-Instruct
444   not handled carefully, can easily cause the model’s output                                                                             XNLI
                                                                                                                                           XNLI_mistral


445   to break down. However, while inputs in different lan-                                                                                                                                                         Original perplexity

      guages may present linguistic differences, they still share
                                                                                                                                            XNLI_qwen

446
447   commonalities as they stem from the same question being                                                                          XQuAD_llama
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          > 400: 0.49%


448   input in the same model. X Transplant skillfully exploits
449
      both these differences and commonalities, allowing the                                                                             XQuAD
                                                                                                                                      XQuAD_mistral


      model to benefit from the broader multilingual knowledge
450
      (differences) while ensuring that the feed-forward activa-
                                                                                                                                       XQuAD_qwen


451
      tions from other languages remain compatible and do not                                                                          XCOPA_llama
                                                                                                                                                                                                                          > 400: 0.09%
452   disrupt the model’s output (commonalities). The results
453   in Figure 5, showing the perplexity distribution under all                                                                         XCOPA
                                                                                                                                      XCOPA_mistral


454   N 2 transplantation strategies alongside the model’s orig-
      inal average perplexity, demonstrate X Transplant’s relia-
                                                                                                                                       XCOPA_qwen
455                                                                                                                                                                                                                               Perplexity
456   bility and stability (see details in Appendix C.3). More-                                                                                           00          50
                                                                                                                                                                      50     100
                                                                                                                                                                             100      150
                                                                                                                                                                                      150      200
                                                                                                                                                                                               200       250
                                                                                                                                                                                                         250       300
                                                                                                                                                                                                                   300     350
                                                                                                                                                                                                                           350       400
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     400       450   500   550


457   over, X Transplant limits the modification of intermedi-
458   ate activations to N 2 possible choices (or even narrows it                                                                           Figure 5: The perplexity distribution
459   down to N , as discussed in Section 5.3), which, compared                                                                             under all N 2 X Transplant strategies

      to making arbitrary changes to hidden states, ensures that                                                                            across different LLMs and datasets,
      the impact of X Transplant on the model’s output remains                                                                              compared with the models’ original per-

483                     Transplant the feed-forward activations from 17th layer (Chinese) to 5th layer (English), then continue the propagation (XTransplant)

484
485
      Figure 6: A intermediate decoding case study of transplanting the feed forward activations from
      Chinese to English, compared with its original responses when prompting in Chinese and English.


                                                                                                                   9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      The example question in Figure 6 is a real case from the GlobalOpinionQA dataset, with all re-
487   sponses generated by LLaMA-2-7B-Chat. We present the model’s responses for the Ask-in-Chinese
488   prompt, Ask-in-English prompt, and a response selected from the N 2 answer space of X Transplant
489   from Chinese to English. As shown, when prompted in Chinese, LLaMA-2-7B-Chat, due to its lim-
490   ited proficiency in Chinese, produced a hallucinated response (C) that was not among the given
491   answer options. When prompted in English, LLaMA-2-7B-Chat also provided an incorrect answer
492   (B). However, by checking the intermediate decoding process of Ask-in-English, we found that
493   LLaMA-2-7B-Chat had the potential to produce the correct answer, as highlighted in the brown
494   box. By applying X Transplant from the 17th layer (Chinese) to the 5th layer (English), the feed-
495
      forward activations from Chinese successfully guided the model to give the correct answer (A).
      Nevertheless, as highlighted in purple box, there is also a risk of over-guidance with X Transplant,
496
      where knowledge from the source language may excessively influence the model’s decision.
497
498
499   7   R ELATED W ORK
500
501   Multilingual Capability. Early multilingual models like mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and
502   XLM (Conneau & Lample, 2019) laid the groundwork for extending pretrained models across
503   diverse languages. Recently larger multilingual models, such as Bloom (Scao et al., 2022) and
504
      Mala-500 (Lin et al., 2024), enhance multilingual capabilities through increased scale. Generally,
      multilingual pretraining and finetuning are now the two mainstream methods for improving multi-
505
      lingual performance. Works like Li et al. (2024b) injects multilingual alignment and preserves this
506
      during pretraining. Gao et al. (2024) explored the effect of multilingual pretraining and instruction
507   tuning on the degree of alignment. Models like Sabia (Pires et al., 2023), ChineseLLaMA (Cui
508   et al., 2023), ChineseMixtral (HIT-SCIR, 2024) are products of continuous pretraining on existing
509   English-centric LLMs. Other like BLOOMz (Muennighoff et al., 2022), m-LLaMA (Zhu et al.,
510   2023), Phoenix (Chen et al., 2023) chosen to directly incorporate multilingual data in the supervised
511   finetuning stage to achieve implicit multilingual alignment across languages.
512
513   Cultural Adaptability. Previous studies have shown that current LLMs exhibit poor cultural
514   adaptability (Ramezani & Xu, 2023; Jha et al., 2023; Rao et al., 2024). Solutions towards these
515   culture-aware challenges can be categorized mainly into two approaches: context learning and
516
      training-based. Kovač et al. (2023) studied models’ controllability in inducing cultural perspectives,
      while Wang et al. (2024) improved cultural performance by explicitly prompting LLMs with the
517
      recognition of culture in queries. Rao et al. (2023) developed a framework integrating moral dilem-
518
      mas with principles from various normative ethics formalisms across different levels of abstraction.
519   Rao et al. (2023) developed a framework integrating ethics from diverse cultures. Another line of
520   research involves fine-tuning models on large-scale culturally relevant datasets (Abbasi et al., 2023;
521   Lin & Chen, 2023; Nguyen et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2024), or investing in more balanced multilingual
522   corpus for pretraining (Scao et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024b).
523
      Unlike previous training-based approaches, X Transplant directly modifies the model’s internal ac-
524
      tivations during inference, allowing the model to benefit from both English and non-English inputs.
525   This simple yet promising mechanism marks a new step forward in cross-lingual capability transfer.
526
527
528   8   C ONCLUSION
529
530   In this work, we introduce X Transplant, a probing method that contributes to further unlocking the
531
      multilingual potential of LLMs, as well as their cultural adaptability, by cross-lingual feed-forward
      activations transplantation. Our extensive experiments across four representative LLMs and four
532
      datasets, along with established upper-bound performance, highlight the underutilization of current
533
      LLMs’ multilingual capabilities. Besides that, we find that the feed-forward activations from En-
534   glish can significantly enhance the model’s multilingual performance, while those from non-English
535   languages enable a deeper and more nuanced understanding of culturally specific content. Our addi-
536   tional analysis and discussion further underscore the practical applicability of X Transplant. Overall,
537   our study not only introduces a novel probing method to improve LLMs’ multilingual performance
538   but also offers a deeper understanding of the mechanisms underlying multilingual knowledge trans-
539   fer. We hope that X Transplant will serve as a catalyst for future research, driving continued progress
      in developing more linguistically effective and culturally aware language models.


                                                        10
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


540
      R EPRODUCIBILITY
541
542   For better reproducibility, our implementation code of X Transplant as well as the evaluation scripts
543   are provided as the supplemental materials. And our pilotset versions of XNLI, XQuAD, XCOPA and
544   GlobalOpinionQA datasets are also available with our code as the testbed for other researchers.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


810
      A    P OTENTIAL Q UESTIONS AND E XPLANATIONS
811
812
813        1. What’s the reason for applying X Transplant only when generating the first new to-
814           ken?
815
816                In autoregressive generation, applying X Transplant during the generation of the
817
                   first new token essentially introduces the benefit of feed-forward activations from
                   another language across the entire sequence generation process. This is because
818
                   all subsequent tokens are influenced by the activations cached from earlier steps.
819
                   If X Transplant were applied during the generation of every token, it would be a
820                redundant operation and could even cause the model’s output to break down.
821
822        2. Why apply En → non-En X Transplant in multilingual tasks and non-En → En in
823           culture-aware tasks?
824
825                For the multilingual datasets (XNLI, XQuAD, and XCOPA), all of the questions
826                are linguistically parallel across languages. These datasets assess the model’s
827                multilingual capabilities by asking questions in various languages such as Chi-
828                nese, Spanish, German, French, etc. When posing questions in these non-English
829                languages, we aim for the model to benefit from feed-forward activations derived
830                from English. Therefore, for multilingual tasks, we perform En → non-En
831
                   X Transplant, where questions are asked in non-English languages, and activa-
                   tions from English are transplanted to the non-English languages.
832
833                Regarding the culture-aware dataset, GlobalOpinionQA, all the questions and
834                answers are in English. The purpose of this dataset is to explore how well mod-
835                els respond to questions from different cultural backgrounds within an English
836                context. When asking questions in English, we want the model to leverage feed-
837
                   forward activations from non-English languages to better capture cultural nu-
                   ances. Hence, for culture-aware tasks, we perform non-En → En X Transplant,
838
                   where the questions are in English, but activations from non-English languages
839
                   are transplanted into the English context. For example, when asking a question
840                related to Chinese culture, we ask the question in English but feed-forward acti-
841                vations from Chinese are transplanted to help.
842
843        3. Concern about “the computational cost”.
844
845                It is important to emphasize that the extensive experiments serve to demonstrate
846                the potential of X Transplant as a simple yet promising mechanism for enhancing
847                multilingual capabilities and cross-lingual transfer, without any modifications to
848                the LLM itself.
849                As the first work to explore this mechanism, we felt that the effort spent on a com-
850                prehensive analysis was justified, and we believe it has successfully showcased
851                the substantial potential of X Transplant.
852
853
                   Additionally, while our work involves significant computational expense, we
                   do not intend for future research to replicate this approach in a resource-
854
                   intensive manner. Our goal is to inspire future work that can utilize the
855
                   concept/mechanism of cross-lingual feed-forward activations transplantation to
856                model design or training phases, such as exploring cross-lingual feed-forward in-
857                teractions and connections between different layers or language-specific regions,
858                to enable the model better leverage its intrinsic multilingual knowledge.
859
                   Furthermore, the idea of transplantation is not limited to multilingual or cross-
860
                   lingual scenarios but could also be applied to cross-task or cross-domain settings.
861
862                Lastly, we hope that the large-scale experiments we conducted, along with our
863                subsequent analysis and discussions, can inspire future research and provide
                   valuable insights for the broader research community.


                                                      16
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


864
      B     E XPERIMENTAL D ETAILS
865
866   B.1    DATASETS
867
868   Due to the extensive scale of our experiments, we did not use the full version of each dataset.
869   Instead, we conducted our experiments on pilotsets from each dataset. Specifically, each pilotset
870   was obtained by randomly sampling 50 examples from the samples in each language covered by the
871   full dataset, with the random seed set to random.seed(666). For better reproducibility, these
872   pilotsets will be publicly available along with our code. The detailed information of these pilotsets
873
      is as follows:
874    Involved Languages
875
       XNLI (15): ar, bg, de, el, en, es, fr, hi, ru, sw, th, tr, ur, vi, zh
876
       XQuAD (12): ar, de, el, en, es, hi, ro, ru, th, tr, vi, zh
877    XCOPA (11): en, et, ht, id, it, sw, ta, th, tr, vi, zh
878    GlobalOpinionQA (24): am, ar, bn, de, el, en, es, fr, hi, id, it, ja, ko, nl, pt, ru, sv, sw, tl, tr, uk, ur, vi, zh-CN
879
880    Sample Size (50 samples per language)
881    XNLI: 50 × 15 = 750
882    XQuAD: 50 × 12 = 600
883    XCOPA: 50 × 11 = 550
       GlobalOpinionQA: 50 × 24 = 1200
884
885
886   B.2    E VALUATIONS
887
      The prompts we used for each dataset are listed in Table 3. For each model involved, we apply
888
      greedy decoding strategy and set the max new tokens generated by the model to 20. We used
889
      Accuracy as our evaluation metric, and for different task types within each dataset, we applied the
890   following rules:
891
892   • For Multiple-choice Tasks (Classification): XNLI, XCOPA, and GlobalOpinionQA all belong to
893     the multiple-choice category. For these tasks, a model’s response is considered correct only if it
894     contains the correct option and excludes all other options.
895   • For Question-Answering Tasks (Generation): For the generative task XQuAD, the model’s
896     answer is deemed correct if the gold answer appears in the model’s response.
897
      To ensure better reproducibility, these evaluation scripts will also be made publicly available.
898
899
      B.3    C OMPARATIVE S ETUP
900
901   In our main experiments, we compared the upper bound performance achieved by X Transplant with
902   the models’ original performance, PIM (Parallel Input in Multiple Languages) (Mu et al., 2024)
903   and CoT (Chain of Thought) (Wei et al., 2022b). Below, we provide a detailed introduction to the
904   implementations.
905
906   • Multilingual Capability: For multilingual datasets XNLI, XQuAD, and XCOPA: (1) The mod-
907
        els’ original performance refers to the performance when the same question is asked in different
        languages. (2) PIM En + lang concatenates prompt non-English language following the English
908
        version prompt, with the intention of prompting the model to output responses in corresponding
909
        non-English language. (3) CoT prompts the models with the suffix of “Let’s think step by step”
910     to utilize their further potential. (4) UpperBound En2lang represents the upper bound perfor-
911     mance achieved by X Transplant when transplanting feed-forward activations from English into
912     other languages.
913   • Cultural Adaptability: For the GlobalOpinionQA dataset, which is designed to assess cultural
914     adaptability in an English-speaking context, both the input and output languages are English. (1)
915     The models’ original performance refers to how well the model answers questions related to dif-
916     ferent cultural backgrounds. (2) PIM lang + En concatenates the English version of the prompt
917     after prompts in other non-English language, aiming to have the model continue generating re-
        sponses in English. (3) CoT prompts the models with the suffix of “Let’s think step by step”


                                                                17
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
        to utilize their further potential. (4) UpperBound lang2En represents the upper bound perfor-
919     mance achieved by X Transplant when transplanting feed-forward activations from non-English
920     languages into English.
921   • Detailed implementation of Multilingual SFT: We randomly selected a total of 20,236
922     multilingual instruction pairs from aya dataset (Singh et al., 2024), ensuring language balance,
923     and performed multilingual supervised fine-tuning on our involved three LLMs. The training
924     was conducted on 8 A800-SXM4-80GB with the following settings: batch size=16, epochs=3,
925     learning rate=1.0e-5, warmup ratio=0.1, and bf16=true.
926
927   B.4   C HAIN - OF -T HOUGHT RESULTS
928
929   As a prominent approach to further utilize LLMs, Chain-of-Thought has been examined in our work
930   for its performance in multilingual and culture-aware tasks, with results illustrated in Table 4 and
931   Figure 8.
932   The results indicate that CoT does not appear to be an effective method for further unlocking the
933   model’s potential in multilingual and culture-aware scenarios.
934
935   B.5   E XPLANATION OF ACCURACY IN E NGLISH SUBSET OF XCOPA FOR
936         Q WEN 2-7B-I NSTRUCT
937
938   In Table 1, we notice that the accuracy in the English subset of XCOPA for Qwen2-7B-Instruct is
939   “0.00”. After specifically revisiting Qwen2-7B-Instruct’s responses to the English subset of XCOPA.
940   We found that the “0.00 accuracy” issue stemms from the model’s failure to effectively follow the
941
      instructions in our prompt. The exact prompt we used was:
942    You are assigned to complete a two-category classification task.
943
944    Premise: The girl squeezed her nose.
945    Options: (1) The baby drools on the bib.
946
       (2) The baby soiled his diaper.
947    Please determine which of the two options is more likely to be the cause of the given premise.
948
949    Your Answer:
950
      However, Qwen2-7B-Instruct’s responses are as follows:
951
952    Option 1 (The baby drools on the bib) is less likely to be the cause of ...
953    Option 1, “The audience clapped their hands to the music,” is more likely to be ...
954    Option 1 is more likely to be the result of the given premise. If the man expected the ...
955
       Option 2, “Her opponent felt sorry for her,” is more likely to be the result of ...
       Option 2, The products are made by child labor. \n\n Explanation: The premise states that radicals ...
956    Option 2, “It’s snack time,” is more likely to be the cause of the given ...
957    ...
958
959
      Our evaluation script for XCOPA dataset considers a model’s response correct only if it contains the
      correct option (e.g., (1) or (2)) and excludes all other options. But as you can see above, Qwen-2’s
960
      responses do not match this format, leading to the “0.0 accuracy”.
961
962   To ensure fairness in evaluation, we can not arbitrarily modify our evaluation script based solely on
963   Qwen’s responses on the English subset of the XCOPA dataset. Therefore, we have retained this
964   result in our main experimental table.
965

971   formance in three scenarios: whether it answered correctly in the source language, in the target
      language, and whether a correct answer exists in the N 2 answer space after applying X Transplant

                                                                                                                                                          1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8



987
988    Figure 7: Proportion of all X Transplant outcomes across 8 correctness categories. ✓ and × repre-
989    sent whether the model answered correctly or not under given settings.
990
991
992
       from the source language to the target language. The combination of correctness in these three set-
       tings results in 8 distinct categories. In Figure 7, we present the sample proportions for these eight
993
       categories across three models and four datasets, leading to the following conclusions:
994
995
       X Transplant does not introduce additional mistakes. The results in Figure 7 across three mod-
996    els and four datasets consistently indicate that for questions that the model could correctly answer in
997    the target language (i.e., the language which received feed forward activations from others), a cor-
998    rect answer is always present in the answer space after applying X Transplant, as the corresponding
999    proportions all being 0%. This reveals that when X Transplant is appropriately utilized, it essentially
1000   serves as an enhancement strategy that does not impair the model’s original performance.
1001
1002   X Transplant benefits more when the question can be accurately answered in source language.
1003   The results in Figure 7 indicate that, in most cases, most of the questions that the model answers
1004   correctly using X Transplant are those that could be correctly answered in the source language itself,
1005   regardless of correctness in target language. This demonstrates that feed-forward activations from a
1006
       source language where the model can answer the question correctly help X Transplant achieve better
       cross-lingual enhancement.
1007
1008
1009
       C.2   L AYER - SPECIFIC A NALYSIS
1010
       In Section 5.3, due to the page limit, the figure 4 only present the layer-specific effectiveness results
1011   of LLaMA-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 on XNLI. Complete results across additional
1012   models and datasets can be found in Figure 11 and Figure 10.
1013
1014   C.3   P ERPLEXITY C ALCULATION
1015
1016   The perplexity results in Section 6.1 include the average perplexity of the model under original
1017   conditions, as well as the average perplexity distribution across all N 2 settings of X Transplant,
1018   encompassing 3 LLMs and 3 datasets. Notably, to mitigate the interference caused by overly short
1019   responses, we only included responses with a token length greater than 5 in our statistics.
